lead: Ask anyone what worries them most about moving home and the answer is rarely the boxes or the heavy lifting. It is the fear of something precious being chipped, scratched or broken along the way. As a family-run removals and storage firm in Westerfield near Ipswich, we have spent since 2005 refining how we keep Suffolk families' belongings safe from the first wrap to the final placement. Here is exactly how our crews protect what matters on moving day.

sections.h: Protection Begins With the Right Materials
sections.body:
Good protection is decided long before the heavy lifting starts, in the choice of materials we bring through your door. We have learned over the years that cutting corners on packing supplies is a false economy, because the cost of replacing a damaged heirloom dwarfs the price of a few extra rolls of bubble wrap. So our vans arrive stocked with proper double-walled boxes, clean packing paper, bubble wrap, furniture blankets, mattress covers and sturdy tape.

The reason we favour clean paper over old newspaper is simple: newsprint leaves ink on pale china and light fabrics, and that mark can be just as upsetting as a chip. Double-walled boxes matter too, because they hold their shape under the weight of a fully loaded van rather than buckling and letting their contents shift. Each material has a job, and bringing the correct one for each item is the first line of defence.

We also match the box to the contents, which is something people often get the wrong way round at home. Heavy things like books go in small boxes so they stay liftable, while light, bulky items such as bedding fill the large ones. A box that is too heavy is far more likely to be dropped, and a box packed loose is where breakages hide. Getting this right from the start sets up everything that follows.

sections.h: Wrapping Furniture So Nothing Gets Marked
sections.body:
Furniture takes the most knocks on a move, simply because it is large, awkward and has to be manoeuvred through doorways and around tight corners. Our answer is to wrap it properly before it ever moves an inch. Sofas and armchairs are covered to keep fabric clean and guarded against scuffs, mattresses go into protective covers so they stay fresh, and polished surfaces are blanketed so they cannot rub against anything hard in the van.

Corners and legs are the parts that suffer most, so we pad them with particular care. A table leg that catches a doorframe, or a dresser corner that meets a wall, is where a careless move leaves its mark, and a few minutes of padding prevents it. For anything with a delicate finish, like an antique sideboard or a French-polished table, we go further still, wrapping generously so the surface is never exposed to a hard edge.

Where a piece is simply too large or too fragile to move whole, we dismantle it carefully and keep the fixings safe in a labelled bag taped to the item. Beds, large wardrobes and some tables travel far more safely in parts, and we reassemble them at the other end. In our experience, taking something apart with the right tools is almost always gentler on it than forcing it whole through a space it was never meant to fit.

Protecting your belongings means protecting the buildings they pass through, both the one you are leaving and the one you are moving into. It is no good delivering your furniture in perfect condition if we have scuffed the hallway or chipped the new front door on the way through. So our crews protect the property as a matter of routine, not as an extra you have to ask for.

On a wet Suffolk morning, and there are plenty of those, we lay coverings over floors and carpets to keep mud and damp from being walked through the house. Doorframes that furniture has to squeeze past are padded, and bannisters are guarded against knocks from passing wardrobes. These touches are easy to overlook because, done well, they leave no trace, but they are a real part of how we keep a move tidy from start to finish.

We are just as careful at the new house, where the last thing anyone wants is damage to a home they have only just taken on. The same coverings and padding go down before we carry anything in, and we plan our route through the property so heavy pieces take the safest path. Leaving both homes as we found them is a point of pride for a local family firm that may well see you, or your neighbours, again.

sections.h: Loading the Van to Keep the Load Still
sections.body:
Much of the damage that happens on a move does not happen in the house at all; it happens on the road, when a poorly loaded van lets things shift and bump along the way. That is why how we load is one of the most important parts of protecting your belongings, even though it looks like nothing more than stacking. A well-loaded van turns a collection of separate items into one solid, stable block that simply cannot move.

We load heavy, robust furniture first to build a firm base, then tier boxes and lighter items above, wedging everything so there are no gaps for things to slide into. Fragile boxes are kept well clear of anything that could crush them, and we use straps to hold larger pieces firmly against the sides of the van. Blankets go between items wherever two surfaces might otherwise rub together on a bend or over a bump.

The result is a load that stays exactly where we put it, whether the journey is across Ipswich or out across the county. We also drive accordingly, keeping speeds gentle and corners smooth so nothing is jostled unnecessarily. People sometimes imagine the lifting is the risky part, but a steady van and a tightly packed load are what really keep your possessions intact between the two front doors.

sections.h: Extra Care for Fragile and Valuable Items
sections.body:
Some things deserve more than the standard care, and we always want to know about them in advance. Antiques, heirloom china, artwork, mirrors and anything genuinely irreplaceable get individual attention, because these are the items that cannot simply be bought again. When you tell us about them early, we bring the right materials and plan the load so they travel in the safest spot in the van.

Mirrors and framed pictures are taped across the glass so any breakage is held together rather than scattered, then wrapped and carried upright on their edges, never laid flat where a knock from above can crack them clean across. Delicate ornaments are wrapped individually so no two hard surfaces touch, and hollow pieces are padded inside as well as out. The principle running through all of it is that nothing precious should ever be left to take its chances loose in a box.

Electronics and specialist items, from televisions to a much-loved piano, each have their own approach too, and we are happy to advise on the best way to move anything unusual. Throughout the day your belongings are fully covered while they are in our hands, which gives you genuine peace of mind, but our real aim is that the cover is never needed because the care taken means nothing comes to harm in the first place.

sections.h: Unloading, Placing and the Final Checks
sections.body:
The care we take does not switch off the moment we reach the new house; if anything, the final stretch is where a calm, methodical approach pays off most. We unload in a sensible order, taking boxes and furniture straight to the room marked on the label rather than dumping everything by the door for you to sort out later. That keeps the new home walkable and stops fragile boxes being stacked under heavy ones.

Heavy pieces are set down gently and placed where you actually want them, so you are not heaving a wardrobe across a bedroom on your own that evening. We reassemble what we took apart, check that beds and tables are sound, and make sure the things you will need first, like the kettle and the children's beds, are easy to find rather than buried at the back of a pile. Small considerations like these are what turn a finished move into a comfortable first night.

Before we leave, we take a moment to make sure you are happy with where everything has gone and that nothing has been overlooked on the van. As a family-run Suffolk firm we would always rather spend a few extra minutes getting it right than drive off and leave you wondering. Looking after your belongings, from the first wrap in Westerfield to the last box placed in your new home, is simply how we have worked since 2005.

faqs: What do you use to protect furniture during a move?

We bring furniture blankets, mattress covers, bubble wrap and padding as standard. Sofas and soft furnishings are covered to keep them clean, polished surfaces are blanketed so they cannot rub, and corners and legs are padded where they are most likely to catch a doorframe. Larger or delicate pieces are dismantled and wrapped where that is the gentler option.
How do you stop things getting damaged in the van on the journey?

The key is leaving no gaps. We load heavy furniture first to form a stable base, then tier and wedge boxes so the whole load becomes one solid block that cannot shift. Straps hold larger items in place and blankets sit between surfaces that might rub. We then drive gently, so your belongings stay still all the way to the new home.
Should I tell you about valuable or fragile items in advance?

Yes, please do. Knowing about antiques, mirrors, artwork, a piano or anything irreplaceable lets us bring the right materials and plan where they travel in the van. We give these items individual wrapping and the safest spot in the load, so a quiet word beforehand means we can give them the extra attention they deserve.
Will you protect my floors and doorways too?

Absolutely. We lay coverings over floors and carpets, especially on wet days, and pad doorframes and bannisters that furniture has to pass. We do this at both the old and the new home as a matter of routine, because protecting your belongings also means leaving both properties as tidy as we found them.
